comments:
Summary
This paper addresses automatic measurement of pre-aspiration boundaries in speech and introduces two machine learning approaches: a frame-level feedforward neural network and a segment-level structured prediction model trained with a passive-aggressive update. The authors evaluate on a fairly large annotated Aberystwyth English corpus (5,297 examples), report cross-validation and leave-one-speaker-out results, test cross-language generalization on a Welsh dataset, and demonstrate that the structured model reproduces results of a linguistic analysis with high correlation to manual measurements. Code is made available.

Strengths
- Novel and useful application: to my knowledge this is the first work specifically targeting automatic pre-aspiration boundary measurement; that makes it valuable to the phonetics and speech-processing community.
- Solid dataset size for a phonetic study and a realistic recording setup. Inclusion of LOSO evaluation and cross-language tests strengthens claims about generalization.
- The structured prediction approach is well motivated for boundary estimation (global loss, hand-crafted interval features) and empirically outperforms the frame-based NN by a clear margin.
- The demonstration that automatic measurements can reproduce linguistic-effects (high correlation and similar mixed-effects model outcomes) is an important practical validation.
- Code release is commendable and increases reproducibility.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement
- Limited baseline comparisons: the NN is a weak baseline (shallow feedforward network operating on short windows). Stronger sequence models (e.g., RNN/LSTM, CNNs over longer context, CRF or structured SVM variants) are not compared; without them it is hard to judge whether the structured model’s superiority comes from modeling structure or simply from better feature engineering. Consider adding a recurrent model or a modern sequence labelling baseline.
- Heavy reliance on manually designed feature maps: while effective here, these are task-specific and may limit portability. An ablation study showing which feature groups contribute most would clarify robustness and guide future extensions.
- Detection vs segmentation: the paper assumes the input contains exactly one relevant obstruent and one pre-aspiration interval. A real-world tool should handle absence/presence detection and multiple candidates; the paper notes future work but would benefit from an explicit discussion and preliminary results on detection performance.
- Evaluation metrics and statistical testing: the main metric (percent within various ms tolerances and mean boundary errors) is sensible, but the paper lacks statistical significance testing when comparing models or speaker/language conditions. Confidence intervals or paired tests would strengthen the claims.
- Inter-annotator reliability and task-loss calibration: the structured model’s task loss uses a threshold that relates to manual uncertainty, but the paper does not report inter-transcriber agreement or how the threshold was chosen beyond a validation selection. Including inter-annotator variance would justify chosen tolerances and contextualize the reported absolute errors.
- Error analysis: more detailed qualitative/error analyses would be useful (e.g., breakdown by plosive place, vowel contexts, speaker, SNR, or very short vs long pre-aspiration). This would show where the model fails and help users interpret automated measurements.
- Limited contexts considered: experiments focus on plosive contexts in relatively high-quality recordings. Discussion or results on fricatives, noisier recordings, or conversational speech would help gauge practical applicability.
- Training and runtime details are sparse: training times, inference speed, and complexity (search over all onset/offset pairs) are not provided; these matter for adoption.

Minor issues
- Some parts of the provided text contain repeated sentences/formatting artifacts (likely from the extraction). The final paper should clean these.
- Full specification of the feature map table is referenced but not shown in the provided excerpt; ensure it is included and clearly explained in the final version.

Recommendation for authors
I recommend strengthening the empirical comparisons (add a stronger sequence baseline and an ablation study of features), reporting inter-annotator agreement and statistical tests, and including more detailed error analyses. Addressing detection (presence/absence) and runtime considerations would make the contribution more immediately useful to practitioners.

Overall assessment
This is a well-motivated, original, and practically useful contribution to automatic phonetic measurement. The structured prediction approach is sound and validated on a sizable dataset with meaningful linguistic replication. With the suggested additional baselines, analyses, and clarifications, the work would be a solid resource for phonetics research that requires automated pre-aspiration measurement.
